one_line_takeaway: A protocol + AWS-backed reference implementation (Riftront) that moves agent-driven mutations from direct API calls to intent proposals gated by policy, bounded by ephemeral scoped identities, and logged in a cryptographic evidence chain — demonstrated qualitatively on simulated failure scenarios with 11 ms policy eval and 3,200 mutations/s, but without quantitative safety rates or baselines.
key_finding: In simulated scenarios the governance engine qualitatively behaved as designed: it deterministically rejected a lower-authority agent's "closed" proposal when a human operator concurrently proposed "open"; selected the most-recent, highest-trust intent among equal-authority racing agents without distributed locks; and pre-emptively blocked two context-blind AWS mutations (terminating a compute instance with live downstream dependencies via AWS Config topology, and a traffic-blind cluster capacity reduction) before any state-mutating API dispatch. Determinism: submitting an identical workload of 10,000 concurrent asynchronous proposals produced strictly identical execution contracts, event logs, and derived states across sequential runs. Performance (on an EC2 m5.2xlarge, 8 vCPU/32 GiB, with an RDS PostgreSQL db.m5.2xlarge event store on gp3 EBS at 6,000 IOPS): policy evaluation averaged 11 ms/request, state-derivation p99 latency < 30 ms, sustained peak of 3,200 mutations/second without observed throughput degradation. Critically, the paper reports NO numeric safety success/failure rates (e.g., % of unsafe mutations caged) and NO baseline performance comparison — safety claims are asserted anecdotally per scenario.
core_contribution: OpenKedge is a protocol that redefines state mutation in agentic (LLM-agent-operated) systems as a governed process rather than an immediate side effect of API invocation. Its three claimed own contributions are: (1) an Intent-Governed Mutation Protocol in which actors submit declarative intent proposals evaluated against event-sourced global context and deterministic policy before any execution; (2) Execution-Bound Safety via "execution contracts" — a tuple C=(a,R,T) bounding permitted action, resource scope, and temporal validity — enforced through ephemeral, task-oriented identities (e.g., short-lived AWS STS session policies scoped to a single ARN); and (3) the Intent-to-Execution Evidence Chain (IEEC), a cryptographically hash-linked append-only lineage binding intent, context, policy decision, execution bounds, and outcome, making every mutation deterministically reconstructable. The paper also asserts three formal invariants (execution-event consistency, multi-agent conflict safety, liveness) and an end-to-end mutation-correctness theorem. NOTE: Cedar, OPA, CRDTs, event sourcing, AARM, Anthropic's Claude Code, ReAct, Toolformer, AutoGen/MetaGPT/ChatDev are all cited related/prior work, not contributions of this paper — Cedar and event sourcing are reused as implementation substrate.
threat_model: In-scope threats it structurally mitigates: semantic hallucinations (syntactically valid but contextually destructive actions), temporal asynchrony / stale-state race conditions, multi-agent collisions over shared entities, and authority override (agents circumventing human operational authority). Trusted computing base: the governance pipeline itself — context aggregation, policy engine, IEEC, and state derivation — on reliable infrastructure. Untrusted: all proactive input sources, including human operators, verified agents, and unverified components (zero-trust at the execution boundary). Explicitly out-of-scope: cryptographic compromise of the underlying event log, direct administrative infrastructure tampering, and cognitive attacks such as prompt injection / model jailbreaks — the paper assumes cognitive attacks WILL hijack agent reasoning and instead aims only to contain the resulting blast radius via bounded task-oriented identities.
limitations: Author-stated (Section 9.3): reliance on manually authored deterministic policies (Cedar rules) requiring explicit human foresight; future work is learning-based policy adaptation and formal-verification integration. Reviewer-observed grounding gaps: (1) the "evaluation" gives no quantitative safety metrics — success is described narratively ("correctly rejected", "consistently caged") with no rates, no adversarial coverage numbers; (2) no baseline is benchmarked — Table 1 is a qualitative feature checklist, not an empirical comparison, and the 11 ms / 3,200 mutations/s figures stand alone; (3) the invariants/theorem are stated informally, not machine-verified; (4) scenarios are simulated, not a real production deployment; (5) by its own threat model it does nothing against prompt injection/jailbreaks; (6) it is a single-vendor preprint (OpenKedge.io) with a linked GitHub repo but no reported reproducibility study.

datasets_used: No public/benchmark datasets; evaluation uses hand-crafted simulated failure-mode scenarios against the Riftront reference implementation
A multi-actor operational-state platform (application-level state) reference domain
A simulated AWS cloud-infrastructure domain (EC2 termination, cluster scaling) using AWS Config, Elastic Load Balancing, STS, and CloudWatch Logs
A synthetic determinism workload of 10,000 concurrent asynchronous intent proposals targeting shared entities
quantitative_results: Policy evaluation averaged 11 ms per request under sustained load
Sustained peak of 3,200 mutations per second with no observed throughput degradation
10,000 concurrent asynchronous proposals reproduced byte-identical execution contracts, event logs, and derived states across sequential runs (determinism)
Benchmark hardware: EC2 m5.2xlarge (8 vCPUs, 32 GiB RAM), Intel Xeon Platinum up to 3.1 GHz on AWS Nitro; RDS PostgreSQL db.m5.2xlarge event store on gp3 EBS at 6,000 baseline IOPS
Example Cedar policy constants: forbids agent updates when human owner updated the entity within 3600 s AND agent trust_score < 0.8
No quantitative safety success rates or accuracy numbers are reported for the conflict-arbitration and infrastructure-safety scenarios (results are qualitative)
baselines_compared: Table 1 is a QUALITATIVE feature comparison only (no numeric baseline), covering: API-based systems
No quantitative baseline is benchmarked against OpenKedge for latency, throughput, or safety
